text: Upon entry into the abdomen during diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon observes Kyle pooling within the abdominal cavity and between the mesenteric leaflets.
The surgeon identifies the leaf of Treves and the terminal ileum during the procedure, visually distinguishing these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon reduces the small intestine, visually identifying the presence of an internal hernia within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon reduces the small intestine while walking up the common channel, revealing lymph trapped within the mesenteric leaflets and visible venous congestion.
The surgeon continues to trace the intestinal pathway until reaching the roux limb, then proceeds to the gastrojejunostomy site, visually inspecting the connections and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon reduces the small bowel and systematically inspects the mesenteric area for signs of internal hernias, focusing on the anatomical structures and tissue planes.
The surgeon inspects the area around the jejunojejunostomy, examining between the mesenteric leaflets. The hernia in this region is visibly sutured closed.
The surgeon flips the Roux limb to the left, away from the camera, revealing the space between the Roux mesentery and the transverse colon to identify a Peterson's defect.
The surgeon identifies a large mesenteric defect in the patient's abdominal cavity, visible as a gap within the mesenteric tissue.

The surgeon identifies a Peterson's hernia as the internal hernia responsible for the patient's bowel obstruction, visually confirming its location between the Roux mesentery and the transverse colon.
The surgeon sutures closed the mesenteric defect using a non-absorbable suture, ensuring the closure of the identified internal hernia.
The video shows the completed closure of the Peterson's defect, with sutures visibly securing the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon runs the bowel one last time, visually inspecting it to ensure there are no additional abnormalities or defects.
